coredata.dc:title: Structure and magnetic properties of Gd4(Co,Ti)41 alloys

Sarafidis, C.
Niarchos, D.
Kalogirou, O.
coredata.dc:description: Series of intermetallic compounds are usually obtained by the general formula R m−n T5m+2n , i.e., taking m RT5 unit cells and replacing n R atoms by T–T pairs. The combination (m,n)=(7,3) which corresponds to R4T41 has not been reported yet. In this work we have investigated the possibility to synthesize the Gd4Co41−x Ti x compounds by annealing the stoichiometric as-cast samples in the temperature interval of 1133–1438K. The Gd4Co39Ti2 alloy annealed at 1133K crystallizes into the disordered TbCu7-type structure. The saturation magnetization M s of this sample is 77.3Am2/kg at room temperature and the alloy exhibits uniaxial anisotropy. The Gd4Co39Ti2 alloy annealed at 1438K crystallizes into the disordered Th2Ni17-type hexagonal phase (2:17H) with cell parameters a =8.350(1) and c =8.224(1)Å.

Permanent magnets
Powder metallurgy
X-ray diffraction
Magnetic measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns (Cu Kα) of powder samples of Gd4Co39Ti2 annealed at different temperatures (*, are the reflections of the Th2Ni17-type structure and +, are the reflections of the TbCu7-type structure).
X-ray diffraction patterns (Cu Kα) of random and magnetically aligned (with the alignment direction normal to the sample holder) powder samples of Gd4Co39Ti2 annealed at 1133K.
Thermomagnetic curve of Gd4Co39Ti2 annealed at 1133K.
Magnetization curves at 5 and 300K of magnetically aligned Gd4Co39Ti2 annealed at 1133K with the magnetic field applied parallel and perpendicular to the alignment direction.

article.ce:title: Structure and magnetic properties of Gd4(Co,Ti)41 alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Materials Science, NCSR “Demokritos”, 153 10 Ag. Paraskevi, Attiki, Greece

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, 54 124 Thessaloniki, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Series of intermetallic compounds are usually obtained by the general formula R m−n T5m+2n , i.e., taking m RT5 unit cells and replacing n R atoms by T–T pairs. The combination (m,n)=(7,3) which corresponds to R4T41 has not been reported yet. In this work we have investigated the possibility to synthesize the Gd4Co41−x Ti x compounds by annealing the stoichiometric as-cast samples in the temperature interval of 1133–1438K. The Gd4Co39Ti2 alloy annealed at 1133K crystallizes into the disordered TbCu7-type structure. The saturation magnetization M s of this sample is 77.3Am2/kg at room temperature and the alloy exhibits uniaxial anisotropy. The Gd4Co39Ti2 alloy annealed at 1438K crystallizes into the disordered Th2Ni17-type hexagonal phase (2:17H) with cell parameters a =8.350(1) and c =8.224(1)Å.

ce:section.ce:para: The iron-rich regions of the R–Fe–T system (R=rare earth, T=transition metal) have been intensively studied with the aim to investigate for new magnetic materials with hard magnetic properties even better than those of SmCo5. As early as 1968, Johnson and Smith surveyed the relationships amongst the structures, which can be geometrically derived from CaCu5-type structure (1:5), namely the rhombohedral Th2Zn17 (2:17R), the hexagonal Th2Ni17 (2:17H) and the tetragonal ThMn12 (1:12) [1]. These phases are formed by dumb-bells substitution for the R atoms in the CaCu5-type structure where the amount and the arrangement of the dumb-bell sites are different. Following the Stadelmaier scheme [2], i.e., taking m RT5 unit cells and replacing n R atoms by T–T pairs, one can get intermetallic series described by the general formula R m−n T5m+2n (where R is a rare earth atom and T is a transition metal). In the well-known table given in [2] all possible formulae for different m and n (n/m <1/2) are given, expect of some gaps witch are justified as “less likely to occur”. Meanwhile, it has been shown that some of the gaps actually correspond to viable compounds. Thus, Psycharis et al. have proved that the gap (m,n)=(10,4) corresponds to the R3(Fe,Ti)29 series [3]. Recently, the successful formation of R3(Co,Cr)29 compounds (R=Gd [4] and Sm [5]) has been reported, which indicate that the (m,n)=(10,4) pair is available also for the Co-alloys.

Cadogan have reported a new series, R3(Fe,Si)22, accomplishing the gap (m,n)=(12,3) [6]. Ivanova et al. [7] have reported the formation of a Tb4(Fe,Si)41 compound, which in the Stadelmaier's model corresponds to (m,n)=(7,3). In a previous work we have investigated the alloys with starting nominal composition R4(Fe,Ti)41 (R=Nd, Er; T=Ti, V) [8]. In this work we extend the previous study for the Gd4(Co,Ti)41 alloys.

Alloys with starting composition Gd4Co41−x Ti x (x =1.5, 2.0, 2.5) were prepared by arc melting of pure elements (>99.9wt%), wrapping the ingot in Ta foil and sealing it in evacuated quartz tubes, followed by annealing at T =1133–1438K for 120h, and then quenching in water. X-ray diffraction measurements (XRD, Cu Kα) were performed on randomly and magnetically aligned power samples to determine the phases present and the easy magnetization direction. The samples where characterized by Rietveld analysis. Thermomagnetic (TMA) curves where recorded above room temperature, with a heating rate of 5°/min, and applying a magnetic field of 150Oe. The values of the Curie temperature were derived from dM/dH versus T curves.

First we studied the Gd4Co39Ti2 alloys. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the as-cast sample and the samples annealed at 1438, 1338 and 1233K. The as-cast sample is a mixture of the 2:17H, 1:12, 1:7 and Co phases. The sample annealed at 1233K crystallizes into the disordered TbCu7 phase. For higher annealing temperatures the main phase is that of Th2Ni17-type structure with hexagonal symmetry and space group P63/mmc. A small amount of the 1:12 phases is present in each one of the alloys.

In Fig. 2 , the X-ray diffraction patterns for the powder specimen of Gd4Co39Ti2 (or Gd2Co19.5Ti1) annealed at 1133K are shown. This sample contains an excess amount of Co compared with the stoichiometric ratio of Gd2Co17. The upper trace is the XRD pattern for the oriented sample and the middle trace for a randomly oriented. The lower trace presents the theoretical pattern of GdCo7 (TbCu7-type structure). All the diffraction peaks of the experimental patterns correspond to the peaks of the theoretical pattern including reflection (002), which is overlapped with the (111) reflection of metallic Co (indicated by an asterisk on the corresponding peak). However, the agreement factors obtained from the Rietveld refinement, using the model of Khan [9] for the TbCu7-type structure, are relatively poor (R p =22.4%; R brag =25.5%). In order to improve the refinement procedure we introduced one more site for Co, in the lattice of the TbCu7-type structure, i.e., the 6l site as reported by Psycharis et al. [10]. Thus, the unit cell parameters became a =4.882(3) and c =4.102(7)Å and the occupancy factors 0.63, 0.35, 0.38 and 0.1 for the sites Gd(1a), Co(2c), Co(2e) and Co(6l), respectively. Using that model the agreement factors of the refinement became R p =8.83%, R wp =10.62%, but the R brag factor remained relatively high, 21.32%. More research is needed to clarify the real structure of this sample. All reflections in the upper trace of Fig. 2 for the oriented sample disappear except the (00l) reflections. This indicates that the sample presents uniaxial anisotropy, contrarily to the stable Gd2Co17 compound, which presents planar anisotropy [11].

It has been reported [9] that in the as-cast sample of Gd2Co17 the disordered phase GdCo7 is formed. However, this TbCu7 phase transforms into the hexagonal Th2Ni17-type structure by annealing at higher temperatures and into that of the rombohedral Th2Zn17-type structure by annealing at lower temperatures. The former is stable at high annealing temperature and the latter is stable at lower annealing temperatures. The excess amount of Co in the case of our sample compared with Gd2Co17 changes this rule. At higher temperatures, in the interval of 1333–1433K the Gd4Co39Ti2 crystallizes into the hexagonal Th2Ni17-type structure. The cell parameters calculated by the Rietveld method for the latter case are: a =8.350(1) and c =8.224(1)Å. When the sample is annealed at lower temperatures (1133K) it crystallizes into the TbCu7-type structure. The Gd4Co39.5Ti1.5 alloys annealed at 1133–1233 have the same behavior as the alloys mentioned above (where Ti=2), in other words they crystallize into the TbCu7-type structure. The alloys with the Gd4Co38.5Ti2.5 composition present a polymorphic system.

In Fig. 3 , the thermomagnetic curve of Gd4Co39Ti2 annealed at 1133K is presented. The magnetic transition occurs at T =1075K, which is smaller than that reported for Gd2Co17 (1210K) [12]. The light increase of the magnetization when temperature increases (see Fig. 3) reflects the well-known fact that the combination of Gd and Co produces an intermetallic compound in which the magnetic moments align antiparallel to each other [12]. The same apparent anomaly is reflected also for the anisotropy field, which is higher at room temperature than that at 5K (2.6T at 5K and 3.6T at 300K) and in the case of the saturation magnetization, Fig. 4 (77.27 and 67.14Am2/kg at 300 and 5K, respectively).

The Gd4Co41−x Ti x (x =1.5, 2.0, 2.5) alloys present a polymorphic system depending on the annealing temperature. New phases were not detected for the temperature range they were annealed. For higher annealing temperatures the main phase is that of Th2Ni17-type structure with hexagonal symmetry and space group P63/mmc. Samples annealed at lower temperatures crystallize in the disordered TbCu7 phases and exhibit uniaxial anisotropy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the European Social Fund and the Greek Ministry of Education in the frame of “Pythagoras” 2.2.3.st EPEAEK project.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Q. Johnson, G.S. Smith, Lawrence Radiation Lab. Rept. UCRL-71094, 1968.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proceedings of the 11th International Symposium on Magnetic Anisotropy and Coercivity in Rare-Earth Transition Metal Alloys
Proceedings of the 17th International Workshop on Rare-Earth Magnets and their Applications
